subsections.title: Exception Hierarchy & Best Practices
subsections.keyRule: Throwable → Error (JVM problems, do not catch) and Exception. Exception → Checked (must handle, IO/SQL) and Unchecked/RuntimeException (programming bugs). Best practices: throw specific, catch specific, never swallow, always log, use try-with-resources for closeable resources.

terms.what: try-with-resources automatically closes any AutoCloseable resource when the try block ends — exception or not. Replaces manual finally { resource.close(); }.
terms.why: Forgetting to close resources (connections, streams, files) causes resource leaks. try-with-resources makes closing automatic and safe.
terms.how: try (Connection conn = ds.getConnection(); PreparedStatement ps = conn.prepareStatement(sql)) { ps.execute(); } — conn and ps auto-closed when block exits.
terms.where: Any AutoCloseable: Connection, Statement, InputStream, OutputStream, FileReader, Scanner, any custom resource.
terms.when: Use try-with-resources (Java 7+) instead of try-finally for AutoCloseable resources — cleaner and safer.
terms.issue: Returning inside finally block swallows exceptions — the exception is silently lost and the finally return value wins.
terms.fix: Never return from finally. Never throw from finally (swallows original exception). Finally is only for cleanup.

star.s: Bug where exception details lost — catch block only shows 'Error occurred'.
star.a: catch (Exception e) { log.error('Processing failed: {}', e.getMessage(), e); throw e; } — log with full stack trace.
star.r: Full exception details in logs. try-with-resources

terms.what: Custom exceptions let you create domain-specific exception types with meaningful names and extra context fields.
terms.why: 'RuntimeException: null' tells you nothing. 'UserNotFoundException(userId=101)' tells you exactly what went wrong.
terms.how: public class UserNotFoundException extends RuntimeException { private final Long userId; public UserNotFoundException(Long userId) { super('User not found: ' + userId); this.userId = userId; } }
terms.where: Service layers, domain logic, API error handling. Each domain has specific exceptions: OrderException, PaymentException, ValidationException.
terms.when: Create custom exceptions for: distinguishing error types for different error responses, adding context (which ID failed), documenting expected failure modes.
terms.issue: Creating too many fine-grained exceptions creates a deep hierarchy that is hard to manage. Exception handling becomes complex.
terms.fix: Create exceptions at the right level of granularity. A few clear types per domain are better than 50 micro-exceptions. Always include context in the message.

star.s: All errors return 500 Internal Server Error.
star.a: Custom UserNotFoundException (→ 404), ValidationException (→ 400), BusinessRuleException (→ 422). @ExceptionHandler in Spring maps each to correct HTTP status.

terms.what: Exception chaining wraps the original exception inside a new one using the 'cause' constructor parameter. Preserves the full error context.
terms.why: When you translate a low-level exception (SQLException) to a high-level one (ServiceException), always include the original as cause. Logs show both.
terms.how: catch (SQLException e) { throw new ServiceException('Failed to save order', e); } — log shows ServiceException with cause = SQLException with cause = original DB error.
terms.where: At every layer boundary: DAO catches SQLException, throws DataAccessException(cause). Service catches, throws ServiceException(cause).
terms.when: Always chain exceptions when translating from one layer to another. Never throw new Exception() alone — always include the original cause.
terms.issue: throw new ServiceException(e.getMessage()) — this loses the stack trace of the original exception. Only the message is preserved. Debugging becomes very hard.
terms.fix: Always use throw new ServiceException('context', e) — the 'e' is the cause. Full original stack trace preserved.

star.s: Production error shows 'Failed to process order' with no further detail.
star.a: Found catch (SQLException e) { throw new ServiceException('Failed'); } — cause not chained. Added cause.
star.r: Logs show full DB error details.

terms.what: Spring's @ExceptionHandler in a @ControllerAdvice class catches exceptions thrown from any controller and converts them to appropriate HTTP responses.
terms.why: Without centralized handling, every controller needs its own try-catch for the same exceptions. @ControllerAdvice + @ExceptionHandler centralizes it.
terms.how: @ControllerAdvice class GlobalExceptionHandler { @ExceptionHandler(UserNotFoundException.class) public ResponseEntity<ErrorDTO> handle(UserNotFoundException e) { return ResponseEntity.status(404).body(new ErrorDTO(e.getMessage())); } }

terms.what: Throwable → Error (JVM-level: OOM, SOE — don't catch) and Exception. Exception → RuntimeException (unchecked) and checked exceptions. Checked = compiler forces you to handle. Unchecked = extends RuntimeException, optional handling.
terms.why: Knowing the hierarchy helps choose the right exception type to throw and catch.
terms.how: Checked: IOException, SQLException — must catch or declare throws. Unchecked: NullPointerException, IllegalArgumentException — optional catch. Error: OutOfMemoryError — almost never catch.
terms.where: API design: use checked for recoverable conditions client can handle. Use unchecked for programming errors.
terms.when: Throw checked when caller can meaningfully recover. Throw unchecked (RuntimeException) for programming errors (invalid args, null where not expected).
terms.issue: Over-checked APIs (every method throws IOException, SQLException) force callers into verbose try-catch that just re-throws.
terms.fix: Wrap checked exceptions in unchecked custom exceptions at service layer boundary. Only checked exceptions that callers meaningfully handle.

star.s: Every method throws 5 checked exceptions — callers have to catch all or declare.
star.a: Wrapped in custom ServiceException (RuntimeException). Callers only catch what they can handle.

subsections.title: java.io vs java.nio
subsections.keyRule: java.io = blocking I/O — thread waits during read/write. java.nio = non-blocking I/O — thread can do other work while waiting. For files: use NIO Path/Files API (cleaner than old File). For network: NIO for high-concurrency servers. Always close resources with try-with-resources.

terms.what: java.nio.file.Path and Files class (Java 7+) are the modern way to work with files — cleaner, more powerful than old java.io.File.
terms.why: Old File API was buggy — delete() returns boolean (not exception), no clear error info. NIO Files throws descriptive exceptions.
terms.how: Path path = Path.of('/data/file.txt'); Files.readString(path); Files.writeString(path, content); Files.copy(src, dest); Files.walk(dir) for directory traversal.
terms.where: All modern file operations. Replaces File.exists(), File.mkdir(), File.listFiles() with cleaner APIs.
terms.when: Use NIO Path/Files for all new code. Migrate old File code when you have opportunity.
terms.issue: Files.walk() does not close its stream if exception occurs mid-walk — resource leak.
terms.fix: Always: new InputStreamReader(stream, StandardCharsets.UTF_8). In modern Java: Files.readString(path, StandardCharsets.UTF_8).

star.s: CSV file reads fine on Windows, shows garbage on Linux.
star.a: FileReader was using platform default charset. Changed to Files.newBufferedReader(path, StandardCharsets.UTF_8).
star.r: Same output on all platforms. NIO — Path & Files

terms.what: Serialization converts a Java object to bytes for storage or network transfer. Deserialization converts bytes back to an object.
terms.why: To save object state to a file or send it over a network, it must become bytes first. Serialization does this conversion.
terms.how: Class implements Serializable. ObjectOutputStream oos = new ObjectOutputStream(fos); oos.writeObject(obj). Deserialization: ObjectInputStream ois = ...; MyClass obj = (MyClass) ois.readObject().
terms.where: Session persistence, RMI, legacy distributed systems, Java object caching.
terms.when: Avoid Java native serialization for new code — it is insecure and fragile. Use JSON (Jackson) or Protobuf instead.
terms.issue: Java serialization is a security risk — crafted byte streams can cause remote code execution. CVEs have attacked Java deserialization.
terms.fix: Do not deserialize data from untrusted sources with Java serialization. Use JSON/Protobuf for data interchange. If you must use serialization, use an ObjectInputFilter to whitelist allowed classes. 📌 Note: serialVersionUID field prevents version mismatch errors when the class changes. Always declare it explicitly: private static final long serialVersionUID = 1L;

star.s: Security audit flags Java serialization in user upload endpoint.
star.a: Replaced ObjectInputStream with Jackson JSON deserialization. Validated input schema.
